events.event: King Charles II grants royal patronage to a group of scientists, founding the Royal Society.
The Royal Air Force is formed as a result of the merger between the RFC and the RNAS.
The first anti-nuclear protest human chain is formed between Greenham Common and Aldermaston.
Admiral Nelson ignores orders to withdraw and sinks the Danish fleet at the Battle of Copenhagen.
Argentina invades the Falkland Islands, leading to the Falklands War.
The birth of Sir Michael Gambon, the celebrated British-Irish stage and film actor.
Sir Robert Walpole becomes the first person to hold the office of British Prime Minister.
The birth of Marlon Brando; his influence on British acting technique remains profound.
The birth of Dame Jane Goodall, the British primatologist and anthropologist.
Francis Drake is knighted by Elizabeth I on board the Golden Hind at Deptford.
The North Atlantic Treaty is signed, founding NATO with the UK as a charter member.
The Beatles occupy all top five positions on the US singles chart, a historic moment for British music.
The death of John Winthrop, whose governorship of Massachusetts influenced British colonial expansion.
The birth of James Young Simpson, the Scottish physician who pioneered the use of chloroform.
The birth of John Hartson, the prolific Welsh international and Premier League footballer.
Richard I of England dies from an infected wound while besieging a castle in France.
The birth of Felicity Palmer; the opera singer's work is celebrated across UK concert halls.
The birth of John Ratzenberger; the actor's roles are well known to UK television audiences.
The notorious highwayman Dick Turpin is executed by hanging in York.
The birth of the Romantic poet William Wordsworth in Cockermouth, Cumbria.
The birth of Ravi Shankar; the musician's collaborations with George Harrison influenced British pop.
Isambard Kingdom Brunel's steamship Great Western leaves Bristol for its maiden voyage.
The birth of Jacques Brel; his work has been frequently translated and performed by British artists.
The death of Margaret Thatcher, the first female Prime Minister of the United Kingdom.
The birth of the pioneering civil engineer Isambard Kingdom Brunel in Portsmouth.
The birth of Patty Pravo; the artist's work reached the UK via European music exchanges.
The Prince of Wales marries Camilla Parker Bowles in a civil ceremony at Windsor.
Bananas go on sale in London for the first time in an apothecary's shop.
RMS Titanic departs from Southampton for her maiden and final voyage.
The Good Friday Agreement is signed, bringing an end to the Troubles in Northern Ireland.
The joint coronation of William III and Mary II takes place at Westminster Abbey.
The birth of Jeremy Clarkson, the prominent British broadcaster and motoring journalist.
The birth of Dele Alli, the England international and Premier League footballer.
The Union Flag is adopted by royal decree as the first common flag for Great Britain.
The birth of John Kay; the musician's career is a notable part of the 1960s British-American rock exchange.
Yuri Gagarin becomes the first human in space, an event reported with awe across the UK.
The birth of Guy Fawkes in York, the most famous of the Gunpowder Plot conspirators.
The Roman Catholic Relief Act receives Royal Assent, allowing Catholics to sit in Parliament.
The birth of Samuel Beckett; the Irish author's 'Waiting for Godot' transformed British theatre.
The death of the German-British composer George Frideric Handel in London.
The birth of Peter Capaldi, the Academy Award-winning Scottish actor and director.
The first cordless telephones go on sale in Britain.
Samuel Johnson's 'A Dictionary of the English Language' is published in London.
RMS Titanic sinks in the North Atlantic, with the loss of over 1,500 lives.
The Hillsborough Disaster occurs, leading to 97 deaths and major changes in football stadium safety.
The Battle of Culloden takes place, ending the Jacobite Rising of 1745.
The birth of Charlie Chaplin in London, a pioneer of the silent film era.
The birth of Spike Milligan, the influential comedian and writer for 'The Goon Show'.
Martin Luther's trial begins at the Diet of Worms; the news spreads quickly through English intellectual circles.
The birth of Billy Fury, the successful English rock and roll singer of the 1960s.
The voting age in Britain is lowered from 21 to 18.
The foundation stone of the current St Peter's Basilica is laid; the event was widely documented by British travellers.
The birth of James Drury; the actor's work in Westerns was highly popular with UK TV viewers.
The birth of Rick Moranis; the actor's comedy films are widely known to UK audiences.
The Battle of Lexington and Concord marks the start of the American War of Independence against British rule.
The death of Lord Byron, the English Romantic poet, in Missolonghi, Greece.
The death of Charles Darwin at his home, Down House, in Kent.
Oliver Cromwell forcibly dissolves the Rump Parliament.
The death of Bram Stoker, the author of 'Dracula', who lived and worked in London for many years.
Enoch Powell delivers his controversial 'Rivers of Blood' speech in Birmingham.
The death of Henry VII and the accession of Henry VIII to the throne.
The birth of Princess Elizabeth of York, later Queen Elizabeth II.
The birth of Iggy Pop; the musician's influence is significant within British punk and rock scenes.
Henry VIII is proclaimed King of England.
The steamship Sirius arrives in New York, the first to cross the Atlantic entirely under steam power.
The death of Stephen Lawrence in a racially motivated attack in South East London.
The birth of William Shakespeare in Stratford-upon-Avon.
The death of William Shakespeare and Miguel de Cervantes.
The coronation of Charles II takes place at Westminster Abbey.
The death of Daniel Defoe, the English writer and author of 'Robinson Crusoe'.
The Easter Rising begins in Dublin, a pivotal event in the history of the UK and Ireland.
The Mass Trespass of Kinder Scout takes place, leading to the creation of National Parks.
The birth of Oliver Cromwell, the Lord Protector of the Commonwealth.
Daniel Defoe publishes 'Robinson Crusoe' in London.
British and ANZAC troops land at Gallipoli during the First World War.
The birth of David Hume, the influential Scottish philosopher and historian.
The Duke of York marries Elizabeth Bowes-Lyon at Westminster Abbey.
South Africa's first multi-racial elections begin, a major event monitored by the UK government.
The birth of Mary Wollstonecraft, the English writer and advocate for women's rights.
The Zoological Society of London opens its zoological gardens in Regent's Park.
The birth of Corey Seager; the baseball star's career is followed by the UK's growing MLB fanbase.
Captain James Cook arrives at Botany Bay in Australia.
The Mutiny on the Bounty takes place in the South Pacific.
The birth of Terry Pratchett, the celebrated British fantasy author.
Oxford University agrees to admit female students to examinations for the first time.
The birth of Sir Daniel Day-Lewis, the triple Academy Award-winning British actor.
The wedding of Prince William and Catherine Middleton takes place at Westminster Abbey.
The birth of Simon Kuznets; the economist's work on GDP is fundamental to UK economic policy.
The birth of Jill Clayburgh; the actress's roles were highly regarded by UK film critics.
The Iranian Embassy Siege in London begins.
